text: For the moment it's in a private repo on github, mainly because it's just me messing about with the language definition. At some point when it's useful / stable enough I will open it up, for sure. And yes, the plan is to actually start something commercial in 2022, so I am actively looking for collaborators.

text:
all of Neil Postman’s books are great, my fav is Amusing Ourselves to Death.

The one caveat / bias I always have to remember though is that he has a very very strong point of view that’s very singular. To the point that I feel like its incomplete, so I would take every sentence with a grain of salt. I’ve forced myself to be more socratic and read ‘opposing’ views as well here. Tech + culture takes are always somewhat non-scientific (doesn’t mean they’re wrong, but like with reading nutrition takes…the value is in absorbing multiple opinions & frameworks)

text:
<@UKFNXF0F9> On the point on lock-in, ownership...:

I don't think the problem is actually ownership or control (with a few regulatory exceptions in certain industries), it's more about trust.

the owernship/control/lock-in issue (as real as it is) is something that can be overcome. It's just a hard, long game of building trust which requires time, money and hard work.  I recall a lot more jokes and complaints from backend devs about how silly of an idea Heroku was (at launch) and that they wouldn't concede control for such little value.

A few years of building a brand, trust and filling in the missing features and I now see the same backend devs opting for Heroku on projects left and right, with no concerns around control.

elements.text:

text: One thing I find it is absurd is julia allocate memory in GPU, tracked by a variable in CPU. When garbage collector running in the CPU find the variable out of scope, the corresponding GPU memory will be deallocated. When a variable represent both a chunk of memory in CPU and in GPU, it complicates the programming reasoning. It would be great if you can address the issue. Instead of assuming every line of code runs in the CPU, we need a programming model to fully drive computation from GPU, instead of being driven.

text: <@UKFNXF0F9> I'm assuming that all generated programs will contain a mixture of CPU and GPU instructions, but that the compiler (and not the programmer) specifies what that mix should be. The reasoning about what the code does should be identical, regardless of the mix. It would be interesting to look at a Julia example to see how we could handle it better.

text: Will the price go down though?

elements.text: Will the price go down though?

text:
It is already happening. OpenAI never published their model of GPT-3 and instead selling an "API" access to people. Watson, AzureAI etc had been doing that for long. We should expect companies like DeepMind follow suit as well.

Here is the funny thing(according to me), it is a (somewhat) vicious circle.

Big Company with money -> Can train big model -> Can put hundreds of dollars in compute and marketing alike -> Can bring media, money, attention -> Bigger company -> Can train bigger model...

Rinse and Repeat.

At least that is how I see it. And for that really important topics, such as Neuro Symbolic (or otherwise) models which can become really data efficient, Ethics in AI, Explainability and Interpretability, Alternative computing models for intelligence (apart form RL) etc. are never really surfacing. The reason being the circle I mentioned above.

text: i have zero experience with c++ but i can probably direct you to a few papers and help you digest them. have you looked into co-dfns? it’s a dialect of apl that can compile and run entirely on the gpu and has some kind of parallel by definition tree structure to support that kind of capability
